title: CS Monitor: Dozens of FTCA Excessive-Force Claims Filed Against ICE Nationwide
excerpt: A Christian Science Monitor investigation reviewed more than a dozen Federal Tort Claims Act filings from June–December 2025 documenting injuries — including bruising, lacerations, head wounds, and chokeholds — inflicted by ICE and Border Patrol agents, finding that the surge in interior enforcement operations has produced a corresponding wave of excessive-force claims extending well beyond Minnesota.
content:
The Christian Science Monitor reviewed legal filings submitted under the Federal Tort Claims Act (FTCA) across multiple states from mid-2025 through early 2026, finding a significant pattern of documented injuries attributed to federal immigration agents.

  • More than a dozen FTCA claims reviewed for the investigation — dated June through December 2025 — described ICE and Border Patrol personnel assaulting or detaining people without cause, leaving claimants with bruises, lacerations, and head injuries.
  • Nearly a dozen claimants said they were tackled or thrown to the ground; multiple claimants said federal personnel pushed their knees into detainees' faces, necks, or backs.
  • One man in Fitchburg, Massachusetts alleged that an ICE agent used a carotid chokehold — a potentially fatal technique prohibited under DHS policy except when deadly force is authorized — while the man was sitting in his car.

Civil rights attorneys told the Monitor that their offices were seeing a 'huge wave of people reaching out with excessive force claims,' noting that the scale of urban enforcement operations was unprecedented in creating such sustained contact between federal agents and the general public. The absence of a central database tracking FTCA claims, combined with ICE's failure to release an annual report for 2025, made precise quantification impossible.
mediaPlaceholder: CS Monitor investigation with chart of FTCA claim locations and injury types from 2025-2026
